metadata.title: Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting
metadata.institution: Department of Computer Science, University of Toronto
metadata.abstract_truncated: Deep neural nets with a large number of parameters are very powerful machine learning systems. However, overfitting is a serious problem in such networks. Dropout is a technique for addressing this problem. The key idea is to randomly drop units (along with their connections) from the neural network during training.
key_claims: Dropout randomly drops units during training to prevent co-adaptation and overfitting.
At test time, weights are scaled by the retention probability p to approximate model averaging over 2^n thinned networks.
Dropout outperforms or matches other regularization methods (L2, L1, max-norm, KL-sparsity) across vision, speech, text, and biology benchmarks.
Dropout can be applied to feed-forward nets, convolutional nets, and Restricted Boltzmann Machines.
Marginalizing dropout noise in linear regression yields a modified ridge regularizer; Gaussian multiplicative noise is a viable high-entropy variant.

architecture.description: Dropout is a regularization wrapper applied over any feed-forward or convolutional neural network. No new module topology is introduced — the core change is a per-layer stochastic masking operation during training and a weight-scaling operation at test time.

variants.name: Dropout Feed-Forward Net

layers.name: InputLayer
layers.notes: D_in varies by dataset. MNIST: 784, SVHN: 3072, Reuters: 2000, AltSplicing: 1014.

layers.name: HiddenLayer_l
layers.notes: Repeated for L layers. MNIST experiments use L=2,3,4 with 1024/2048/4096/8192 units.

layers.name: DropoutMask_Hidden_l
layers.operation: element-wise multiply by Bernoulli(p_hidden) mask r^(l)

layers.name: OutputLayer
layers.notes: C = number of classes. Dropout is NOT applied to the output layer.

variants.test_time_modification: All dropout masks removed. Each layer's weight matrix W^(l) is multiplied by p^(l): W_test^(l) = p^(l) * W^(l).
variants.max_norm_constraint: ||w||_2 <= c applied per hidden unit weight vector; c is a tunable hyperparameter, typical values 2–4.

variants.name: Dropout Convolutional Net (SVHN/CIFAR/ImageNet)
variants.notes: Dropout applied to convolutional layers and fully connected layers. For SVHN: 3 conv layers + 2 FC layers, all ReLU, max-pooling after each conv. Retention probs: p=(0.9, 0.75, 0.75, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5) from input to FC layers.

variants.name: Dropout RBM
variants.notes: RBM with visible v in {0,1}^D and hidden h in {0,1}^F. Dropout mask r in {0,1}^F sampled per training case. Units with r_j=0 are forced to h_j=0. CD-1 used for training. See Section 8.

ambiguities.description: Exact architecture for the DBM+dropout finetuning result (0.79% MNIST) — DBM pretraining topology uses 500-500-2000 but finetuning depth/width for the final net is not fully re-stated.
ImageNet architecture is referenced to Krizhevsky et al. 2012 (AlexNet) rather than described in this paper.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: AlexNet: 5 conv layers + 3 FC layers, dropout in last 2 FC layers at p=0.5

symbols.z_i^{(l+1)}: pre-activation of unit i in layer l+1
symbols.w_i^{(l+1)}: weight vector for unit i in layer l+1
symbols.b_i^{(l+1)}: bias of unit i in layer l+1

symbols.r_j^{(l)}: binary mask for unit j in layer l

equations.name: Thinned Layer Output
equations.role: training — element-wise masking of layer output

symbols.r^{(l)}: Bernoulli mask vector for layer l

equations.name: Dropout Forward Pass
equations.role: training — forward pass through thinned network
equations.notes: Applied at every hidden layer. Mask is re-sampled for each training example.

equations.name: Test-Time Weight Scaling
equations.notes: Multiplying by p ensures expected output at test time equals expected output at train time.

equations.name: Max-Norm Regularization Constraint
equations.role: training constraint applied via projection after each gradient step

symbols.w: incoming weight vector at a hidden unit

equations.name: Marginalized Dropout for Linear Regression
equations.role: theoretical analysis — shows dropout ≡ ridge regression with input-scaled regularizer
equations.notes: Derived in Section 9.1. Shows dropout implicitly applies stronger regularization to high-variance input dimensions.

equations.name: Multiplicative Gaussian Noise (Gaussian Dropout)
equations.role: alternative to Bernoulli dropout; no test-time weight scaling needed
equations.notes: Same mean (1) and variance ((1-p)/p) as Bernoulli dropout scaled version; higher entropy.

equations.name: Dropout RBM Joint Distribution
equations.latex: P(r, h, v; p, \theta) = P(r; p) P(h, v | r; \theta)
equations.role: generalization of dropout to Restricted Boltzmann Machines

symbols.r: binary dropout mask vector for hidden units

tensors.name: r^(l) (dropout mask)
tensors.notes: Re-sampled per training example per forward pass. NOT applied at test time.

tensors.name: y_tilde^(l) (thinned output)
tensors.role: intermediate — input to layer l+1 during training

tensors.name: logits / softmax output
tensors.role: output — C is number of classes

optimizer_hyperparams.learning_rate: 10–100x that of equivalent non-dropout net; decayed over training
optimizer_hyperparams.lr_schedule: annealed (specific decay formula in TIMIT: lr_0*(1 + t/T)^{-1})
optimizer_hyperparams.weight_decay: L2 optionally combined; max-norm is preferred with dropout

dropout.input_layer: p = 0.8 for real-valued inputs (image patches, speech frames)

max_norm.applied_via: project w onto L2 ball of radius c after each gradient step

training_pipeline.batch_size: mini-batch SGD; exact batch sizes not universally specified

training_duration.MNIST: 1 million weight updates; hyperparams tuned on 10K validation split then retrained on full 60K
training_duration.SVHN: trained until best validation error; val set not merged back due to large training set

pretraining.weight_scaling_on_finetune: pretrained weights scaled up by 1/p before dropout finetuning
pretraining.finetuning_lr: smaller than random-init lr to preserve pretrained weights

training_pipeline.loss_function: cross-entropy (classification); code-quality / neg-KL for Alternative Splicing; MSE implied for regression analysis

data_augmentation.MNIST: none for permutation-invariant setting; distortions possible in non-PI setting
data_augmentation.SVHN: global mean/variance normalization per channel; no ZCA or GCN (no improvement)

datasets.name: SVHN
datasets.task: digit recognition from street view images

datasets.name: Alternative Splicing
datasets.task: predict probability of 3 splicing events across 4 tissue types
datasets.metric: Code Quality (negative KL divergence, higher is better)

key_results.model: Dropout NN + max-norm, ReLU, 3 layers 1024 units
Dropout NN + max-norm, ReLU, 2 layers 8192 units

implementation_assumptions.assumption: PyTorch is the target framework (torch.nn.Dropout module applies Bernoulli mask and rescales by 1/p at train time — equivalent to paper's test-time weight scaling when inverted)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Modern standard practice; paper used CUDA-C (cudamat/cuda-convnet) but PyTorch is the contemporary equivalent

implementation_assumptions.assumption: PyTorch nn.Dropout uses 'inverted dropout' convention: scales by 1/p during training rather than p at test time. Both are equivalent. Implementation should note this difference from paper notation.
implementation_assumptions.basis: PyTorch documentation; mathematical equivalence with appropriate lr scaling
alternatives: Manual Bernoulli mask + test-time scaling as in paper

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Xavier/Glorot initialization for weights unless pretraining is used
implementation_assumptions.basis: Standard practice for ReLU nets; paper uses random init but doesn't specify strategy

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Activation function is ReLU for primary experiments (paper shows ReLU outperforms logistic on MNIST: 1.35% → 1.25%)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Explicitly compared in paper; ReLU used in SVHN, CIFAR, TIMIT best results

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Max-norm projection implemented as: w = w * min(1, c / ||w||_2) after each gradient step
implementation_assumptions.basis: Standard implementation of max-norm constraint; paper states project onto L2 ball of radius c

implementation_assumptions.assumption: Batch size of 128 for MNIST experiments (not specified in paper)
implementation_assumptions.basis: Common practice for MNIST with SGD; paper only states mini-batch SGD

implementation_assumptions.assumption: For the MNIST reproducibility demo, we target: 2-layer ReLU net with 1024 units, p=0.5 hidden, p=0.8 input, max-norm c=2, momentum=0.95, aiming for ~1.06% error
implementation_assumptions.basis: Explicitly reported configuration in Table 2 and Appendix B.1

ambiguities.description: Exact initial learning rate values not given universally — only the heuristic of '10–100x the non-dropout optimal lr'. TIMIT gives lr=0.1; MNIST does not specify a number.
ambiguities.primary_assumption: lr=0.01 for MNIST (within typical range for SGD on MNIST)

ambiguities.description: Exact batch size for MNIST/CIFAR/SVHN experiments not stated in the paper.
ImageNet architecture not described here; deferred to Krizhevsky et al. 2012 (AlexNet).
ambiguities.primary_assumption: AlexNet architecture with dropout in last 2 FC layers

reproducibility_scope.primary_target: MNIST classification — Dropout Feed-Forward Net with ReLU and max-norm (Table 2 row: 1.06% error, 3-layer 1024-unit net)
reproducibility_scope.tertiary_target: Effect of dropout rate (Figure 9) and data set size (Figure 10)
